item.title: It’s a cycle
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
I took a moment to watch this productivity video and it made me think and I found myself debating so I think that is good.

What I found most helpful was to think about the the types of information that we deal with

  • Tasks

  • Ideas

  • Notes

  • Resources

These are similar concepts found in other systems notably GTD.

C = Capture

Getting items out of your head and into a system.

O = Organize

Processing what has been captured. Keep track of its state-Not started, In Progress, Completed, etc.

R = Review

Updating the capture list and keeping its status current and identify what needs to be done.

E = Engage

Doing the work.

I asked Claude

What happens if I don’t finish in the first engage cycle?

The cycle starts again.

Capture → Organize → Review → Engage → (new info) → Capture → Organize → Review → Engage

The cycle ends when the task is completed.

item.title: Qualtrics Fun
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description: Ran into trouble with Qualtrics and display logic this week. Qualtrics had trouble pulling into the drop downs and created havoc. I knew to use an older version but I need to use the published version to resolve my issue.

item.title: An OJS Primer
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
Kevin
item.description:
Okay, I need the primer on OJS; I liked the flexibility of setting up the input values but I am not feeling the syntax of the plots.

This Repo helped me figure out setting up the input values using a data frame including correcting converting the data. The quarto page helped me figure out how to filter the data.

Claude provided a conversion steps from ggplot2 to Observable JS plot.

Yes, you can convert a ggplot2 script to an Observable JS plot. Here’s an approach to translate ggplot2 concepts to Observable JS using the Plot library:

import * as Plot from "@observablehq/plot";

// Assuming similar data structure to R/ggplot2
const data = [
  {category: "A", value: 10},
  {category: "B", value: 15},
  {category: "C", value: 12}
];

// Equivalent to ggplot(data, aes(x=category, y=value)) + geom_bar(stat="identity")
const barPlot = Plot.plot({
  marks: [
    Plot.barY(data, {
      x: "category", 
      y: "value",
      fill: "category"
    })
  ],
  x: {label: "Category"},
  y: {label: "Value"},
  color: {legend: true},
  title: "Bar Plot Conversion"
});

// Scatter plot equivalent
// ggplot(data, aes(x=x, y=y, color=category)) + geom_point()
const scatterPlot = Plot.plot({
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(data, {
      x: "x", 
      y: "y",
      fill: "category"
    })
  ],
  color: {legend: true}
});

// Line plot equivalent
// ggplot(data, aes(x=x, y=y, group=category)) + geom_line()
const linePlot = Plot.plot({
  marks: [
    Plot.line(data, {
      x: "x",
      y: "y",
      stroke: "category"
    })
  ]
});

// Render the plot
document.body.append(barPlot);

Key conversion tips: - ggplot() → Plot.plot() - geom_bar() → Plot.barY() - geom_point() → Plot.dot() - geom_line() → Plot.line() - Aesthetic mappings (aes) become configuration objects - Styling and legends are handled similarly but with different syntax

The main differences are in the configuration approach and method names. Observable JS Plot is more functional and uses method chaining differently from ggplot2’s layered approach.

item.title: Data Equity
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
Kevin
item.description: I learned about this Series last week and looks like a great opportunity for professional development.

item.title: Process Culture
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
Kevin
item.description:
More or less process needed?

This posteevie from Seth Godin has some key quotes for me to think about. At first, I thought it was a justification for process culture, but not exactly. There is more nuance in that being 100% focused on process may not produce breakthroughs, but an organization without any process will flame out.

Process is the investment we make in inefficiency now to prevent errors from costing us later.

Godin provides the example of an emergency room; it is process-focused but not very efficient, but the system does not rely on good luck.

item.title: Google Sheets Tables
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
Kevin
item.description: At my day job, I have been incorporating tables with my Google Sheets. I like the setup and the aesthetic of the table format. I wanted to brush up on how to analyze the data from a table. My favorite Google Sheets YouTuber has a video and I highly recommend his other videos.

item.title: Knowledge Work
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
Kevin
item.description:
This Post quote captures my work. I wish I could embed the post.

I see knowledge work as sophisticated decision making weighing trade offs of unpredictable outcomes judged by irrational humans who disagree with you and each other.

Our agency has to set up other agencies and individual to do their piece of the puzzle well; the manual can’t cover everything and there are often irrational humans involved. When there is disagreement there needs to be negotiation.

item.title: Customize Shiny Manger Log In Screen
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
Kevin Gilds
item.description:
I use Shiny Manager Package to provide basic authentication for a Shiny App. This post is about the customizing the style the log in page.

Many years ago, I received feedback with the suggestion to change the default message. However, I could not figure out how to make this change.

However, I took another stab at it and was able to figure it out with the help of Claude.ai. I

shinymanager::set_labels(
    language = "en",
    "Please authenticate" = "Please log in"
)

The required change was easy but not obvious to me. I placed this code in the run_app.R function in my Shiny Application

item.title: Saved by Qualtrics Version Control
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
Kevin Gilds
item.description:
By saved, I mean being able to revert back a survey saved me countless hours as I could have lost around 96 points of display logic. One night this week, I was adding another display logic point and Qualtrics was acting funny and by funny I mean determining that all my previous display logic was no longer valid.

I thought this cant’ be serious and when I previewed my survey; I knew the situation was dire. Thankfully, I recalled Qualtrics has Version history and I was able to revert my survey to a point where all the display logic was working correctly.

Here the general steps to revert a survey back to a working version.

1). While in Survey Builder

2). Go to Tools

3). Select Versions

4.) Find the point where the survey was how you need it.

Once I reverted the survey back to an acceptable state, I added the new display logic. I am now very thankful for the survey version feature.

item.title: Elmer
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
Kevin Gilds
item.description:
This Article is a good primer on the structure of LLMs and use cases.

For me, the gap between the idea and production is speeding up for a Shiny Application that I maintain–thanks to LLMs.

I wanted to improve a section in my application that displayed the count of surveys entered by site. My prompt for Shiny Assistant was not great and got this Toy Application. Did I replicate this? No but it set me in motion on what could be done.

item.title: RSS Feeds
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
I use newsboat and Feeder as RSS services and wanted to add this blog. I determined I needed to touch up the settins my website to accomplish this task.

Guidance could be found here at the Quarto Website

I needed to add this to my quarto yaml.

website:
  title: "myblog"
  site-url: https://www.myblogexample.io
  description: "A great sample blog"
  navbar:
    right:
      - icon: rss
        href: index.xml
  • Please note I needed to reference blog.xml rather than index.xml

Below needed to be added to main index.qmd file.

---
title: "myblog"
listing:
  contents: posts
  sort: "date desc"
  type: default
  categories: true
  feed: true
---

I have been using Positron and noticed it is a bit different workflow and its confusing because after a preview I can push new content. I noticed my feed was not being created. I went back to Rstudio to rebuild the site. After, I rebuilt in Rstudio my feed was created and I could subscribe to my own content.

item.title: Shiny Fun
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
I was working on new section of a Shiny Application and wanted to put two tables side by side without creating a new box within the tabpanel.

My random idea was just two insert a column function in the box; however, when I added the column I received an error. I added two columns but the tables were still stacking on top of each within the row. Clause AI suggested to wrap the two column in a fluidRow statement and it worked like a charm.

 fluidRow(  # Add this fluidRow
      column(
        width = 6,
        h3("Pre Outcome Site Report"),
        gt::gt_output(ns("pre_site_report_23"))
      ),
      
      column(
        width = 6,
        h3("Pre Process Site Report"),
        gt::gt_output(ns("pre_site_process_report_23"))
      )
    )  # Close the fluidRow

item.title: All Equal()
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
Today I need to find a difference between two data frames. Actually, I had to split the data frame and find the difference between two survey entries.

## Create lists
df_list <- split(dupes, dupes$response_id)


### Split the list as save a data frame
df_1 <- as.tibble(df_list[1])

df_2 <- as.tibble(df_list[2])
all.equal(df_1, df_2)

Compares the date frame and I could easily tell there were many differences between these two entries. If I had time I would use fake data. Here is the 📦documentation

item.title: Cron-Jobs-GHA
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
I scheduled my first GHA with a cron job. The idea was to set it for 5:00 am every Sunday.

I was happy this morning that my GHA ran but was surprised that it ran early than expected. I realized that I need to factor in time zones and that my schedule was set for UTC. Claude A.I. was able to help me reset it for Eastern Standard time.

item.title: Stylying a Shiny App
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
I was running into a issue of formatting a shiny application. In a tab panel, the content was running into the left margin. I asked Claude A.I and it gave me 3 options.

I chose the following and it worked like a charm.

div(
    style = "margin: 20px;",     # Or use padding: 20px;
    # Your plots and tables here
  )

item.title: Test Your SQL
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
Introduction

SQL is on my mind and this looks like an interesting resource to test those SQL skills. I struggle with new data sets so this may be a good challenge.

I found the resource from this post on bluesky.

Starting my new data analyst job next week and wanted to have a refresh of SQL.

Ran through the SQL Murder Mystery (mystery.knightlab.com) and SQL Island (sql-island.informatik.uni-kl.de).

Nice confidence boost seeing how much quicker and more efficient I was compared to a few months ago

[image or embed]

— Tim (@talbtree.bsky.social) November 4, 2024 at 3:46 PM

item.title: Github Actions
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
Introduction

I spent the weekend revisiting how to build a GitHub Action to automatically pull data from Clockify. It was a challenging experience that felt like my first time coding due to a series of small, avoidable mistakes. I had to piece together information from multiple sources and use ChatGPT to get my YAML formatted correctly.

Learning

The announcement of this book and its chapter on GitHub Actions inspired me to start this project again. I found this project workflow especially helpful.

  1. One useful takeaway was the idea of using a DESCRIPTION file for projects, even if they’re not packages. I used an renv workflow, but encountered challenges installing the Rcpp package on the remote machine. I wonder if the setup with a DESCRIPTION file might have prevented some of these issues.

  2. If you’re writing data to your repository, you need to provide write permissions. GitHub offers a default token (GITHUB_TOKEN) for GitHub Actions, which simplifies this process. Initially, I tried using my own PAT, but thanks to ChatGPT, I learned to switch to the default GITHUB_TOKEN instead:

    Tip: GitHub provides a default GITHUB_TOKEN for Actions that usually works for most repositories, eliminating the need for a custom PAT. Replace any custom PAT reference in the git-auto-commit-action step with ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}.

Conclusion

I worked through several challenges, and hopefully, the next time I attempt this, it won’t be quite as difficult.

item.title: Links
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
Weekly Wrap-up

I updated my R version to 4.4.1–I suppressed the pain involved in updates such as re-installing packages. What has been fun is I don’t appear to be able to deploy my Shiny App to ShinyApps.io and it is not clear to me why.

Items

Useful R items:

I have been waiting for this–How to secure static html files. This link https://nikitoshina.quarto.pub/quarto_encryption/ provides an example on how to do this. The tool to secure the file is JavaScript and it is required to have Node.js installed on your computer. This has the potential to be a game changer.

Useful R 📦 newpost; it uses the Rstudio API to create a new folder and starts a new post for you. This seems to work a bit better than my add-on.

Browser based Postgress database. I am not sure of my use case with this.

Fin

Working to get back in the habit of posting more.

item.title: Flywheels
item.dc:creator: Kevin Gilds, MPA
item.description:
I liked this (Post). The insight is that we notice when someone is not very good or great at a skill. This may be overstated but there is some truth to it.

The same semantic applies when someone assumes someone was born to code or understands data.

I can still feel the awkwardness of learning to code–it felt unnatural.

What is great about learning to code/how computers work there is a the flywheel effect in that once you understand something many concepts fall into place.
